skippedSources.name: TMAD Flow
skippedSources.reason: Minimal content — algorithmic picks switch, no analysis

items.title: Gold miners at one of the deepest discounts to the S&P 500 on record — fundamentals strong, prices falling
items.idea: Tavi Costa flags that gold miners are trading at historic discounts to the S&P 500 despite strong fundamentals. Prices keep falling even as the macro case strengthens — a divergence he reads as an accumulation phase for smart money. Broader macro thesis centers on interest rates and the US dollar.
items.angle: The miner-to-gold ratio and miner-to-SPX ratio divergence at extremes is historically a contrarian entry signal. If dollar weakens and rates soften, miners are the leveraged bet. $GDX $GDXJ.
items.tweetA: Gold miners are trading at one of the deepest discounts to the S&P 500 ever recorded. Fundamentals: strong. Prices: falling. Either smart money is quietly loading up on the most hated trade on earth, or the macro thesis is broken. I know which one Tavi Costa thinks it is.
items.tweetB: The setup in gold miners is brutal in the best way. Stocks down, fundamentals intact, discount to SPX near record. If you believe in the dollar thesis AND rates eventually rolling — this is the leveraged bet. $GDX $GDXJ. Tavi Costa flagging this now.

charts.desc: Charts on interest rates and USD thesis

items.title: US Redbook same-store retail sales: +10% YoY for week ending Jun 20 — exceptional strength continues
items.idea: Redbook Index running at +10% YoY (prior: +9.4%), significantly above pre-AI-boom trend. US consumer is more resilient than regional survey data implies.
items.angle: Consumer strength at +10% YoY while market prices 3 rate hikes is combustible. Strong consumer = Fed has room to hike. Inflation stays elevated. Dollar strengthens. Podolsky's thesis.
items.tweetA: US Redbook same-store retail sales: +10% YoY. Week ending June 20. This is running at nearly double the pre-AI-boom trend. The consumer is not slowing. The Fed sees this too. BofA's call for 3 hikes this year is looking less outlandish by the day.
items.tweetB: Everyone is watching semis collapse. Meanwhile US retail sales are running at +10% YoY. That is the actual tension. Economy hot. Fed constrained. You cannot have ripping consumer spending AND easy money. Something has to give — and historically it is equities that blink first.

items.title: JPMorgan Cembalest: 'The US is the most vibrant and prepared country for AI, with China close behind on some measures'
items.idea: Michael Cembalest at JPMorgan links US productivity leadership in G10 to AI preparedness. US leading, China close behind on some measures — a rare acknowledgment from a major US institution that the gap is not as wide as consensus assumes.
items.angle: US equity premium is a productivity and AI story, not just valuation. Dollar follows. China closer than consensus implies on some dimensions.
items.tweetA: JPMorgan's Cembalest: US productivity growth is leading the G10 and AI explains why. The US is the most vibrant and prepared country for AI. China close behind on some measures. A major bank openly saying the gap is not as wide as most assume.
items.tweetB: If productivity is the scoreboard and AI is the game, the G10 chart is clear: US winning, China closer than consensus thinks, everyone else falling back. Dollar at 52-week highs is the market pricing this correctly. Or maybe too correctly.

items.title: DXY vs SPX late-90s parallel: global capital flooding into US assets again — stocks AND dollar rising together
items.idea: JC Parets at TrendLabs draws explicit parallel to late 1990s: global investors want US stocks, US tech, US dollars. DXY at new 52-week high, US equities outperforming by wide margin. Late 90s episode ran 4+ years before violent unwind.
items.angle: Dollar + stocks rising together is the late-90s signature. Buy US assets aggressively, exit when dollar peaks. Not there yet. Intermediate-term bullish for US equities and DXY.
items.tweetA: Dollar at 52-week highs. S&P 500 leading global equities. JC Parets is asking: is this the second half of the 1990s again? Then too, the world wanted US stocks AND US dollars. That trade ran for 4 years before it broke.
items.tweetB: When dollar and stocks go up together, it is a signal of capital concentration. Late 90s. Happening again. The question is not whether to own US assets. It is whether you will have the timing discipline to exit when the capital tide eventually turns.

charts.desc: DXY vs SPX overlay showing late-90s parallel (TrendLabs)

items.title: Equal-weight Tech outpacing cap-weight Tech by widest margin since the pandemic bubble of early 2021
items.idea: Per DataTrek, equal-weighted large-cap Tech index outpacing cap-weighted by widest spread since speculative bubble of early 2021. Smaller tech names participating, not just Mag 7. In early 2021, this dynamic ended badly — speculative layer unwound violently in Feb-March.
items.angle: Equal-weight outperforming cap-weight in tech = speculative layer firing. Late-cycle breadth phenomenon. Looks bullish until the speculative cohort corrects sharply.
items.tweetA: Equal-weighted Tech is beating cap-weighted Tech by the widest margin since early 2021. The last time this happened, the speculative layer blew up two months later. Breadth looks great until it stops looking great — and when it does in this setup, it moves fast.
items.tweetB: Hidden in the tech selloff: equal-weight vs cap-weight spread at the widest since the 2021 bubble. Smaller tech names ran harder than Mag 7 into this correction. When the mean reverts, it hits the speculative names first. Watch $RSPT for the tell.

items.title: Memory's share of hyperscaler capex: 35% in 2026 → 48% by 2027 (SemiAnalysis / CLSA)
items.idea: Per SemiAnalysis and CLSA, memory is rapidly becoming a larger share of hyperscaler capital expenditure: 35% in 2026 climbing to 48% in 2027. Structural shift in AI buildout from compute (GPUs) toward memory (HBM, DRAM) as the constraining bottleneck.
items.angle: Memory at 48% of hyperscaler capex reframes the Korea KOSPI selloff in SK Hynix as valuation reset, not demand reversal. Micron earnings are a read on the single largest line item in AI infrastructure spend by 2027.
items.tweetA: Per SemiAnalysis/CLSA: memory share of hyperscaler capex goes from 35% in 2026 to 48% in 2027. The AI buildout is shifting from GPU to memory as the primary spend. Which makes the Micron selloff look less like a demand concern and more like a valuation reset at the wrong time.
items.tweetB: Memory at 48% of hyperscaler capex by 2027. Nearly half of all AI infrastructure spend going to one category. The Korean KOSPI crash was not a thesis break — it was a positioning flush. The underlying demand story is getting bigger, not smaller. $MU $SNDK

items.source: The Dark Side of the Boom
items.title: AI trade now requires near-perfect execution: hyperscalers spending, suppliers beating, inflation behaving, Fed patient, dollar stable — all simultaneously
items.idea: The AI trade now demands a flawless multi-variable sequence every quarter. The Nomura (McElligott) framework explains the amplification: once the market turns, dealer hedging + leveraged ETF rebalancing + systematic de-risking converts a modest earnings reassessment into a mechanical global selloff.
items.angle: Not 'is AI real?' Everyone agrees. The question is can a $10T+ capital cycle deliver near-perfect results without interruption. History says no. Position accordingly.
items.tweetA: The AI trade no longer asks you to believe in AI. It asks you to believe that an enormous capital cycle can deliver near-perfect execution — every quarter, every capex print, every demand signal — without a single meaningful pause. That is a much harder thing to believe.
items.tweetB: Five things must go right simultaneously for the AI trade to hold: hyperscalers keep spending, suppliers keep beating, inflation behaves, Fed stays patient, dollar does not tighten. Each is probable. All five at once, quarter after quarter? That is the actual risk embedded in these valuations.

items.title: Korea KOSPI -10% cascade: Nasdaq -2.2%, SOX -7.9%, Sandisk -13.6%, Micron -13.2% — Micron earnings now make-or-break
items.idea: South Korea KOSPI fell -10% from record highs, cascading: PHLX Semiconductor Index -7.9%, Sandisk -13.64%, Micron -13.18%, LRCX -9.33%. SPX -1.4%, Nasdaq -2.2%, DJIA -0.1%. All 12 tech stocks down 8%+ were still up double digits YTD. Micron earnings after tomorrow's close is the critical test.
items.angle: DJIA -0.1% while Nasdaq -2.2% is the tell. Precise repricing of AI-leveraged growth, not broad risk-off. Micron is the referendum on whether this was positioning or thesis.
items.tweetA: Sandisk -13.6%. Micron -13.2%. SOX -7.9%. Nasdaq -2.2%. Dow -0.1%. That last number is the tell. This was not broad risk-off. It was a precise repricing of AI-leveraged growth at exactly the stocks that had doubled in 6 months. Micron earnings tomorrow resets the narrative.
items.tweetB: Korea KOSPI -10% and US semiconductor index drops 8% the same day. That is a transmission mechanism. Leveraged Korean retail, SK Hynix, HBM, Micron — all connected. The question is whether this flush creates an entry or reveals a crack. Micron guide will answer it.

items.title: Cerebras first post-IPO earnings: poor market reaction sets up Micron as the single most important AI memory data point
items.idea: Cerebras reported its first earnings since IPO with a negative market reaction, per Neil Sethi. Two consecutive AI-adjacent earnings disappointments in one week would materially shift sentiment in the semiconductor complex heading into Q3.
items.angle: Cerebras disappoints → Micron carries the entire burden of the AI memory narrative. One-way positive reactions to AI earnings are over. This is the new regime.
items.tweetA: Cerebras first post-IPO earnings: weak reaction. Micron reports tomorrow. Two AI-adjacent earnings in one week with bad market reactions would change the entire conversation about whether AI infrastructure spend is translating into earnings power.
items.tweetB: The era of AI stocks popping 10%+ on any earnings beat is ending. Cerebras disappointed. Micron is up next. The market used to forgive everything with a 'buy the guide.' If Micron lands flat or negative, the narrative shifts from 'dip buyer' to 'wait and see.'

items.title: BofA warns Fed raises rates 3 times in 2026 — new Chair Warsh at helm; oil above $100 on Iran risk
items.idea: Bank of America issued a note warning the Fed will raise rates three times in 2026, driven by oil above $100 on Iran supply risk reigniting inflation. New Fed Chair Kevin Warsh said he did not feel need to raise at latest FOMC but BofA says it is only a matter of time. Dollar already at 52-week high.
items.angle: Three Fed hikes is the new tail risk the market is just starting to price. With dollar at 52-week highs and tech selling off, this is the Podolsky scenario: short-term yields up, dollar stronger, stocks wobble then vault. The wobble is now.
items.tweetA: BofA just put out a note: the Fed raises rates 3 times in 2026. Oil above $100 on Iran risk, dollar at 52-week high, new Fed Chair Warsh who idolizes Volcker. This is not a remote scenario. The market is still pricing cuts. Those two things cannot both be right.
items.tweetB: Buried in the TheStreet note: the Micron-Anthropic supply deal announced Monday went 'largely ignored.' That is the tell. Six months ago that headline would have been +10% for $MU. Now it barely moves the needle. Sentiment shifts before fundamentals do.

items.title: Google DeepMind loses Noam Shazeer (Attention Is All You Need) to OpenAI; Nobel laureate Jumper to Anthropic; Nvidia acqui-hires Ashish Vaswani
items.idea: In days: Google DeepMind lost Noam Shazeer (2017 Transformer paper co-author) to OpenAI; Nobel laureate John Jumper (AlphaFold) to Anthropic. Nvidia acqui-hired Essential AI team including Transformer co-author Ashish Vaswani. OpenAI/Anthropic using pre-IPO equity + compute allocations as currency. Deeper driver per Axios: short window before recursive self-improvement begins.
items.angle: Google is losing the people who invented the technology Google is built on. If the next 18-24 months are decisive before models improve themselves, talent concentration at OpenAI/Anthropic accelerates their lead. This is the real AI race.
items.tweetA: Google DeepMind lost Noam Shazeer — co-author of Attention Is All You Need, the paper that created ChatGPT — to OpenAI. Same week. Lost Nobel laureate John Jumper to Anthropic. Nvidia grabbed Ashish Vaswani. Google is losing the people who invented the technology it runs on.
items.tweetB: Why are top AI researchers leaving Google for OpenAI and Anthropic? Not just money. It is compute allocation and timing. Many believe there is a short window before models improve themselves. Everyone wants to be at the lab they think will win that race. Google's size is now a handicap.

items.title: SpaceX 'Elon Web Services' emerges: Anthropic + Google $70B+ deals, plus Reflection AI $6B open-source deal backed by Nvidia
items.idea: Elon Musk monetizing excess AI infrastructure: combined $70B+ with Anthropic and Google (cancellable at short notice), plus new ~$150M/month (~$6B over 2-3 years) deal with Nvidia-backed Reflection AI for open-source training. Michael Parekh labels this 'EWS' (Elon Web Services). SpaceX/xAI becoming a major AI cloud provider without hyperscaler branding.
items.angle: SpaceX becoming a shadow hyperscaler. Nvidia funds the deal that gives SpaceX its revenue. Everyone's incentives align until they don't. The AI compute supply chain now runs through Musk.
items.tweetA: Elon Musk is quietly building 'Elon Web Services.' Anthropic + Google: $70B+ in AI infrastructure deals. Reflection AI: $6B deal at $150M/month. All cancellable at short notice. AWS took 15 years to build. Elon is assembling the same thing in 18 months using excess xAI capacity.
items.tweetB: Nvidia backed a startup called Reflection AI. Then SpaceX gave Reflection $6B in compute. Then Jensen and Elon both win: Jensen advances open-source AI in the US, Elon monetizes idle compute. This is what the AI supply chain actually looks like now. It runs through Musk.

items.title: G7 in French Alps: Trump flanked by Altman + Hassabis; Macron flanked by Amodei + Benioff — AI CEOs as quasi heads of state
items.idea: At G7, AI lab CEOs sat at bilateral photo positions reserved for presidents: Trump flanked by OpenAI's Sam Altman and Google DeepMind's Demis Hassabis; Macron flanked by Dario Amodei and Marc Benioff. No precedent in prior tech waves. Altman warned 'not to cede your responsibilities to AI labs like mine.' Amodei urged democracies to 'resist the temptation to splinter.'
items.angle: Sovereign AI is a geopolitical category now. Direct implications for EU AI Act, China strategy, and US export controls. The lobbying is just getting started.
items.tweetA: At G7: Trump flanked by Sam Altman and Demis Hassabis. Macron flanked by Dario Amodei and Marc Benioff. AI CEOs in the chair usually reserved for heads of state. Altman told the room: do not cede your responsibilities to AI labs like mine. They are now powerful enough to give that warning and mean it.
items.tweetB: Sovereign AI is a geopolitical category now. G7 summit, bilateral photos with AI CEOs standing where prime ministers stand. This is not PR. It is the recognition that whoever controls frontier AI controls something as strategic as nuclear capability. The lobbying is just getting started.

items.title: Meta $299 AI smart glasses own-branded (no Ray-Ban): 7M+ units sold; Google Android XR coming later 2026
items.idea: Meta launched cheaper own-branded AI glasses at $299, dropping Ray-Ban, with Gen-2-equivalent features minus display. Google Android XR/Gentle Monster coming later this year. Amazon and Apple in pipeline. 7M+ units sold industry-wide. Key unresolved issue: societal permission for camera use without the social cue of a held phone.
items.angle: The $299 price signals volume adoption push before competitors arrive. Core friction — privacy and social trust — does not resolve with a price cut. Killer use case remains the open-ear audio. Not the camera. Not the AI.
items.tweetA: Meta just dropped own-branded AI glasses at $299. No Ray-Ban. 7M+ units sold industry-wide. Google coming this year. Amazon and Apple in the mix. The hardware race is real. The unresolved problem: society has not granted social permission to record people through glasses. No price cut fixes that.
items.tweetB: The best feature on Meta's $299 AI glasses is open-ear Bluetooth headphones. The camera is a novelty nobody uses publicly. The AI assistant is redundant with your phone. At $299 you are paying for the ecosystem bet and hoping the killer use case emerges. That is the honest take.

items.title: Ethereum Foundation cuts 20% staff (54 employees), restructures into 5 clusters — Vitalik endorses Bitcoin-like ossification
items.idea: EF laid off ~20% (54 employees), reorganized into 5 domain clusters: Protocol, Access, User, Community, Institutional. PSE (Privacy and Scaling Explorations) dissolving as standalone pod. Vitalik advocates 'soft lean-and-done ossification' once Strawmap roadmap is complete — trending toward mostly security fixes, fewer new features. More like Bitcoin. ETH at $1.6k (-4%).
items.angle: Ossification is a massive long-term thesis shift. Less protocol risk, more predictability, higher institutional confidence. Short-term negative (layoffs). Long-term bullish: stable protocol is what institutions need before building on it.
items.tweetA: Ethereum Foundation just cut 20% of staff and Vitalik publicly endorsed ossification — trending toward a Bitcoin-like model once the Strawmap roadmap is done. More security fixes, fewer new features. ETH down 4% on the day. I think the market is reading this exactly backwards.
items.tweetB: The EF laying off 54 people and PSE dissolving sounds bearish. But Vitalik's ossification thesis is actually bullish for $ETH long-term. Institutional developers want a stable, predictable protocol. What the EF is describing is Ethereum growing up. New independent orgs like Ethlabs filling the gaps.

items.title: Vitalik: EF shifting from client diversity as redundancy to AI-assisted formal verification as primary security paradigm
items.idea: Vitalik highlighted a fundamental shift in Ethereum's security approach: from client diversity (multiple clients = no single point of failure) to AI-assisted formal verification as the new primary security paradigm. AI dramatically reduces the cost and time to apply formal verification at scale — can mathematically prove code correctness.
items.angle: AI-assisted formal verification could dramatically reduce smart contract risk at the protocol level. Ethereum becomes a different risk profile for builders and institutions. Watch for first formal verification announcements from EF's new structure.
items.tweetA: Vitalik today: Ethereum is moving from client diversity as redundancy strategy to AI-assisted formal verification as primary security. Formal verification can mathematically prove code correctness. AI makes it cheap enough to apply everywhere. Game changer if they pull it off.
items.tweetB: The most interesting line from Vitalik on EF restructuring: AI-assisted formal verification is replacing client diversity as Ethereum's core security model. Ethereum's moat in the long run is not decentralization theater — it is mathematical proof of correctness. Completely different sell.

items.title: Meta building 'Arena' — standalone prediction markets app (Polymarket/Kalshi competitor), points initially, real stakes later
items.idea: Per NYT, Meta is quietly building standalone prediction markets app 'Arena.' Users initially wager points, real money possible later. Direct competition with Polymarket and Kalshi. Meta's distribution advantage (3B+ users) makes this a potential category killer.
items.angle: Meta entering prediction markets is the most interesting Web3-adjacent product move in years. If Arena launches with real money staking, competes directly with Polymarket's $1B+ in trading volume. Either a death blow to Polymarket or validation that the category has arrived.
items.tweetA: Meta is quietly building 'Arena' — a standalone prediction markets app. Points-based first, real money later. NYT broke the story. $META has 3B+ users and infinite distribution. Polymarket has better crypto rails. Kalshi has the regulatory moat. This just got interesting.
items.tweetB: Meta entering prediction markets with 'Arena' is the mainstream legitimization moment the category needed. The question is whether they do it through crypto rails or fiat. The answer tells you whether Web3 wins the prediction market wars or loses them to Meta's distribution.

items.title: Allium closes $40M Series B — notable as Dune laid off 25%, Messari acquired at fraction of 2022 valuation
items.idea: Crypto data startup Allium closed $40M Series B (Amplify Partners) — significant given the analytics space collapse: Dune laid off 25% in May 2026, Messari acquired by Blockworks at fraction of 2022 valuation. Allium's raise suggests bifurcation: enterprise-grade data infrastructure winning while consumer-grade analytics struggle.
items.angle: Flight to quality in a downturn. Infra wins over applications in bear markets. Same as every tech cycle — AWS outlasted the dot-coms. Know which side you are on.
items.tweetA: Dune laid off 25%. Messari sold for a fraction of 2022 value. And Allium just raised $40M Series B. The crypto data space is bifurcating: enterprise-grade infra is winning, consumer-grade analytics are losing. Same as every tech downturn — infrastructure survives. Applications wait for the next cycle.
items.tweetB: The Allium $40M raise while Dune and Messari struggled is a signal. Institutional data infrastructure is still getting funded. Retail-facing analytics are getting marked down. The same AWS-vs-dot-coms playbook is playing out in crypto data. Know which side you are on.

items.source: The Dark Side of the Boom
items.title: ETF inflows hit $1 trillion YTD — 45% above last year's record pace; historical full-year average was $490B
items.idea: ETF inflows have already topped $1 trillion year to date in 2026, running ~45% above last year's record pace. Historical full-year average was ~$490B. In under 6 months, investors committed more than twice the historical annual average. Most channeled into same narrow band of concentrated winners.
items.angle: When passive inflows double the annual average in half a year and concentrate into 10 stocks, the vulnerability is mechanical not fundamental. Same flows that created the upside become forced sellers on the way down. Korea was a preview.
items.tweetA: ETF inflows in 2026: $1 trillion in less than 6 months. Full-year average is $490B. We have already done 2x a full year. Almost all going into the same 10 stocks. The upside from passive concentration is well understood. The downside mechanics are what Korea just demonstrated.
items.tweetB: I keep coming back to this number: $1 trillion in ETF inflows in under 6 months, running 45% above last year's record. That is an ocean of passive capital, 40 cents of every S&P dollar going to the top 10 names. It works beautifully until it doesn't. And when it doesn't, the exit is the story.

charts.desc: ETF inflows YTD vs historical annual averages

items.source: The Dark Side of the Boom
items.title: Leveraged ETF assets at record $218B (+60% since March); tech leverage +136%, semiconductor leverage tripled
items.idea: Leveraged ETF assets climbed to record $218B, up ~$82B (+60%) since end of March 2026 alone. Tech-linked leverage +136%, semiconductor-linked leverage nearly tripled. This amplifier turned the Korean positioning event into a US market cascade — leveraged ETF rebalancing forces selling into weakness mechanically.
items.angle: Semiconductor leveraged ETFs tripling in 3 months is why Korea had such large wings in the US. When semis fall, levered ETFs sell more semis. Doom loop. At $218B and still growing, the system is now large enough to move markets.
items.tweetA: Semiconductor-linked leveraged ETF assets nearly tripled since end of March. Total leveraged ETF assets at record $218B, up 60% in 3 months. This is why Korea's retail leverage could move global markets. When these products rebalance into weakness, they sell what just fell the most. The loop.
items.tweetB: The market structure story nobody is talking about: $218B in leveraged ETFs, tech leverage up 136% and semis tripled since March. This is a mechanical amplifier. Every correction gets bigger than fundamentals justify because the rebalancing sells into the weakness. Circle that number.

charts.desc: Leveraged ETF assets by sector 2026 YTD (record $218B)

items.source: The Dark Side of the Boom
items.title: 40 cents of every S&P dollar goes to top 10 stocks; 33 cents to Mag 7 alone — index concentration at historic extreme
items.idea: ~18 cents of every dollar into S&P 500 directed to semiconductor companies, 33 cents to Magnificent Seven, ~40 cents to top 10 stocks. More capital funneled into fewer destinations than at any recent point. When the top names sold off, there was no offsetting bid from other constituents who receive so little capital.
items.angle: 40 cents into 10 stocks is the structural vulnerability of passive investing at scale. The index was designed to diversify — at current concentration levels it does the opposite. Watch equal-weight vs cap-weight as the leading indicator of any rebalancing.
items.tweetA: 40 cents of every dollar flowing into the S&P 500 goes to the top 10 stocks. 33 cents to the Mag 7 alone. This is not diversification — it is a concentrated momentum bet wearing the label of passive investing. When it unwinds, it unwinds all at once.
items.tweetB: The S&P 500 was designed to give you 500 companies. Today you effectively get 10. 40 cents of every dollar into the top 10 names. The rest gets crumbs. This is why Korea could move global markets — concentration creates a system where the dominant names are both the only bid and the only sell.

charts.desc: S&P 500 concentration chart — % of flows to top 10 vs historical

items.source: The Dark Side of the Boom
items.title: Circle July 1: new quarter triggers flood of fresh passive capital — retirement, target-date, mutual funds all deploy simultaneously
items.idea: Citadel's Scott Rubner flagged June 17 that technicals would matter more than fundamentals into month-end. Post-fog, July 1 begins new allocation cycle: retirement contributions, target-date funds, passive mandates, mutual-fund inflows, systematic strategies all deploy fresh Q3 capital simultaneously. ETFs now ~31% of avg daily volume (up from 27% 10Y avg). The mechanical bid could overwhelm any remaining fundamental concerns.
items.angle: Setup into July 1: technical selloff into quarter-end (mechanical selling) followed by mechanical bid from quarter-start flows. If Micron earnings are neutral-to-good, July 1 flow + Q3 fresh capital could produce a sharp reversal.
items.tweetA: Circle July 1. Retirement contributions, target-date funds, passive mandates, and systematic strategies all deploy fresh Q3 capital simultaneously. ETFs are now 31% of daily volume. That mechanical bid into a technically oversold market could look like a 'recovery.' It is mostly plumbing.
items.tweetB: Markets often bounce hard after quarter-end technical selloffs. July 1 is the start of the new allocation cycle — $490B+ in annual ETF inflows does not come in evenly. It front-loads at quarter-start. The volatility into June 30 may be setting up one of those mechanical bounces. Watch the open July 1.

items.source: The Dark Side of the Boom
items.title: BTC and gold sold alongside stocks; bonds caught bid — signals broad leveraged risk reduction, not a growth scare or macro trade
items.idea: In the Korea selloff session: bonds and dollar caught a bid while Bitcoin and gold were sold. This eliminates simple macro narratives — not a pure growth scare, not inflation hedge unwind, not crypto flight to safety. It was broad reduction in leveraged risk appetite: everything bought with borrowed capital sold simultaneously.
items.angle: BTC and gold falling together with equities is the fingerprint of a leverage event, not a fundamental repricing. Recovery should be equally mechanical once leverage is flushed.
items.tweetA: In yesterday's Korea selloff: bonds rallied, dollar rallied, BTC fell, gold fell. That is not a growth scare. That is not an inflation read. That is deleveraging. When BTC and gold both sell with equities, leveraged portfolios are being unwound across all asset classes simultaneously.
items.tweetB: BTC down 3%, gold down, bonds up, dollar up. Fingerprint of a pure leverage flush. No macro thesis. Just forced selling across everything bought with borrowed money. These events tend to reverse as fast as they happen, once the margin calls clear. Watch for the snapback.

items.title: AI capex ~$1 trillion next 12 months (+30% YoY) = 3% of US GDP; combined with 5%+ deficit = 9% total public/private stimulus
items.idea: Paul Podolsky puts AI capex in fiscal context: AI spending grows ~30% to ~$1T next 12 months = 3% of US GDP. Add 5%+ federal budget deficit = roughly 9% of GDP in combined public/private stimulus. Critical difference from 1999: the US was running a budget surplus during the last tech frenzy. Today the government also has its foot on the gas.
items.angle: 9% of GDP in combined stimulus while the Fed is pressured toward rate hikes is the fuel equation. Market cannot fall far with this much fiscal impulse. But inflation also cannot fall far. Resolution: higher rates, higher dollar, wobble, then melt-up.
items.tweetA: AI capex next 12 months: ~$1 trillion = 3% of US GDP. Plus 5%+ federal deficit. Paul Podolsky's math: the US is running roughly 9% of GDP in combined public/private stimulus. The last time we had a tech boom this big, the government was in surplus. This time it is also flooding the economy with cash.
items.tweetB: In 1999 during the last tech bubble, the US had a budget surplus. Today we have a 5% deficit AND $1 trillion in AI capex. 9% total stimulus while the Fed is debating rate hikes. That is not a recipe for a crash. It is a recipe for inflation, a Volcker-style hike, and then a melt-up. We are in chapter one.

charts.desc: US budget deficit chart contrasting 1999 surplus vs 2026 deficit

items.title: 1999 analog: Fed hiked → stocks -12% → then +110%; Podolsky expects Warsh to hike this summer or September
items.idea: Podolsky draws explicit 1999 parallel: when Fed first raised rates during tech frenzy, stocks fell 12% then rose 110%. He expects Kevin Warsh to make a Volcker-style move — early, proactive hike to assert credibility. Forecast: higher short-term yields, stronger dollar, market wobble, then vault higher. Swiss franc already weakening on expectations of tighter US policy.
items.angle: 1999 hike-then-rip pattern is what Podolsky is betting on. If Warsh hikes in September, initial reaction is -12%. Then market rediscovers 9% fiscal stimulus and $1T AI capex. Painful intermission. It may be starting now.
items.tweetA: Paul Podolsky's call: Warsh hikes this summer or September, Volcker-style. 1999 playbook: Fed hikes during tech frenzy, stocks fall 12%, then rise 110%. Expects higher yields, stronger dollar, a market wobble — then a vault higher. The wobble might be happening right now.
items.tweetB: Swiss franc is weakening. It yields zero. The only reason to sell CHF is if you expect US rates to rise and US real yields to climb. FX market pricing Warsh hikes before equity market has fully priced it. That spread between FX and equity does not last. One of them is wrong.

items.title: The loop: stocks won't fall until households stop buying; households won't stop while earnings strong; earnings won't weaken without a policy shock
items.idea: Podolsky identifies the feedback loop preventing a clean correction: stocks won't fall until households stop buying; households won't stop because earnings are strong; earnings won't weaken without a rate hike; rate hike won't happen until Warsh makes a Volcker move. The only exit is an exogenous policy shock.
items.angle: Be long risk assets until the policy signal is unambiguous, then position for the -12% before the +110%. The system sustains itself until Warsh breaks it.
items.tweetA: Podolsky's loop: stocks won't fall until consumers stop buying. Consumers won't stop because earnings are strong. Earnings won't weaken without a rate hike. A rate hike won't happen until Warsh makes a Volcker move. The market is stuck in this loop until he breaks it. He probably will.
items.tweetB: The reason the market cannot correct cleanly: every dip is bought by consumers flush with income from strong labor markets, sustained by AI capex and fiscal stimulus. The only circuit breaker is a policy shock. Nothing breaks naturally in a 9%-of-GDP-stimulus environment.

items.title: Margin debt expansion indicator at bear market warning level — called 3 major peaks in 3 decades; three versions of the chart all agree
items.idea: Callum Thomas at ChartStorm (Topdown Charts): margin debt expansion indicator is at bear market warning levels, having preceded three major market peaks in 30 years. Three versions of the chart all give the same warning: raw margin debt expansion; margin debt growth minus S&P 500 growth (strips out price effect); and net margin debt as % of market cap (just broke above historical range top). Best signal when indicator reaches extreme AND turns — at the extreme but turn not yet confirmed.
items.angle: Margin debt warning + leveraged ETFs at record + ETF concentration + equal-weight tech breadth at 2021-bubble levels = multiple independent indicators pointing the same direction. Any one can be dismissed. All four together is worth paying attention to.
items.tweetA: ChartStorm's margin debt expansion indicator just hit bear market warning territory. This signal called 3 major peaks in 30 years. Stack it: leveraged ETF assets at record $218B, ETF inflows $1T YTD, top-10 concentration at 40 cents per dollar. The setup is historically unusual.
items.tweetB: I keep waiting for the margin debt chart to be dismissed because 'stocks going up causes margin to go up.' Callum Thomas already ran the math: margin debt growth MINUS S&P 500 growth still shows an extreme. The leverage is real, not just a function of price appreciation. The warning stands.

charts.desc: Three versions of margin debt warning chart: raw, adjusted for S&P returns, and as % of market cap — all at extreme levels

items.title: Micron-Anthropic supply deal announced Monday went 'largely ignored' — the good-news-stops-working sentiment shift
items.idea: TheStreet notes the Micron-Anthropic supply deal announced Monday 'went largely ignored' — a stark sentiment regime change signal. Six months ago, any Micron deal with an AI hyperscaler would have been +10%+. The deal is bullish for memory demand. The market ignoring it means investors are no longer rewarding AI-demand confirmation — focused instead on valuation and execution risk.
items.angle: Good news not being rewarded is the most reliable late-cycle signal in markets. When positive catalysts stop producing positive returns, the marginal buyer is gone. Position accordingly for Micron earnings.
items.tweetA: Micron announced a supply deal with Anthropic on Monday. The stock barely moved. Six months ago that would have been a 10% day. When good news stops working, the marginal buyer is gone. That is the most important signal from this week's AI selloff and it was hiding in a footnote.
items.tweetB: The Anthropic-Micron supply deal was real and bullish for memory demand. The market ignored it. Then Micron fell 13% on Korea contagion. This is the regime where fundamentals are secondary to positioning and sentiment. The news was good. The reaction was wrong. It told you everything.

items.title: SpaceX: $600B market cap wiped out after IPO debut — warning shot for OpenAI and Anthropic IPO pipeline
items.idea: TheStreet reports SpaceX has seen a $600 billion market cap wipeout since its IPO debut, testing investor patience. SpaceX is simultaneously the AI compute provider via xAI/EWS structure. OpenAI and Anthropic are planning mega-IPOs later in 2026 at astronomical multiples.
items.angle: SpaceX losing $600B post-IPO is a warning shot for the OpenAI and Anthropic IPOs. If the market can mark down SpaceX by $600B after debut, frontier AI lab IPOs pricing at even higher multiples carry serious post-IPO risk regardless of fundamentals.
items.tweetA: SpaceX: $600 billion market cap wiped out since IPO debut. OpenAI and Anthropic are planning mega-IPOs later this year at even higher valuations. The market just told you what it does to trillion-dollar science projects once they stop being narratives and start being public stocks. Pay attention.
items.tweetB: SpaceX -$600B post-IPO. Cerebras: weak earnings reaction. Micron: ignored a deal with Anthropic then fell 13%. The window when AI companies could do no wrong in public markets may be closing faster than the IPO pipeline is ready for. Altman and Amodei are watching this very carefully.

items.title: PMI divergence: composite at 5-month high (manufacturing 49-month high) while Philly Fed services contracting; ADP employment near highs
items.idea: Data split on June 23: Philly Fed services weak and contracting, Richmond manufacturing soft. But composite PMI at 5-month high, manufacturing PMI at 49-month high, price pressures cooling. ADP employment near highs. The market ignored all of it — too busy repricing leveraged tech. But the underlying data is not recessionary.
items.angle: Manufacturing PMI at 49-month high while semis sell off 8% in a day is a remarkable divergence. Real economy accelerating while financial economy reprices leverage. This is not 2022. This is AI-capex-driven manufacturing revival running underneath a positioning correction.
items.tweetA: US manufacturing PMI hit a 49-month high yesterday. The same day the semiconductor index fell 8%. The real economy and the financial economy are moving in completely opposite directions. That divergence tells you this is a positioning event, not a macro deterioration. The manufacturing data is the tell.
items.tweetB: Composite PMI at 5-month high. ADP employment near highs. Redbook retail +10% YoY. And the Nasdaq fell 2.2% on Korean retail margin calls. The US economy is not breaking. The leveraged positioning in AI stocks is breaking. These are very different problems with very different solutions.
